source_paper_title: A Simple Integral Equation Approach for Optimal Investment Stopping Problems with Partial Information
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Xing_2026, title={A Simple Integral Equation Approach for Optimal Investment Stopping Problems with Partial Information}, volume={51}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0268}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0268}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Xing, Jie and Ma, Jingtang and Zheng, Harry}, year={2026}, month=jan, pages={542–567} }
context:
Page 18, Section 6 (Conclusions)

There remain some problems, for example, the current approach can not be extended if the drift coefficient is a hidden two-state Markov chain.
title: Extend dimension-reduction integral-equation method to Wonham-filter drift uncertainty setting
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-time financial market on a filtered probability space supporting a risky asset price process \(S\) with volatility \(\sigma>0\), and an unobservable drift process \(\mu_t\) taking two values \(\mu_h>\mu_l\). The agent observes only the price filtration \(\mathcal F^S_t\) generated by \(S\), not \(\mu_t\) nor the driving Brownian motion.

In the paper’s baseline model, \(\mu\) is an \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable two-point random variable and the conditional probability \(P_t=\mathbb P(\mu=\mu_h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) satisfies a Bayesian-filter SDE of the form \(dP_t=\Theta P_t(1-P_t)d\widetilde B_t\), where \(\Theta=(\mu_h-\mu_l)/\sigma\) and \(\widetilde B\) is the innovation Brownian motion. The authors then introduce the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\) and a measure change \(\mathbb Q\) under which \(\Phi\) becomes a geometric Brownian motion, enabling a dimension reduction that converts an intractable free-boundary integral equation involving \(\mathbb E[\cdot]\) under the joint law of \((Y_t,P_t)\) into a solvable integral equation without unknown joint transition densities.

In the alternative setting highlighted in the conclusion, the unobservable drift is instead a hidden two-state continuous-time Markov chain \(\alpha_t\in\{h,l\}\) with generator
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
-\lambda_h & \lambda_h\\
\lambda_l & -\lambda_l
\end{pmatrix},\qquad \lambda_h,\lambda_l>0,
\]
and \(\mu_t=\mu(\alpha_t)\in\{\mu_h,\mu_l\}\). The conditional probability \(P_t:=\mathbb P(\alpha_t=h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) then follows the Wonham filter
\[
dP_t = (\lambda_l-(\lambda_h+\lambda_l)P_t)\,dt + \Theta P_t(1-P_t)\,d\widetilde B_t,
\]
with innovation process \(d\widetilde B_t = \sigma^{-1}dS_t/S_t - \mathbb E[\mu(\alpha_t)\mid\mathcal F^S_t]dt\).
Define again the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\). Under a measure \(\mathbb Q\) analogous to the one used for the Bayesian-filter case, \(\Phi\) satisfies an SDE of the form
\[
d\Phi_t = (\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)\,dt + \Theta\Phi_t\,dW_t,
\]
for a \(\mathbb Q\)-Brownian motion \(W\). In the Bayesian-filter case, a key identity enabling dimension reduction is \(F_t=\frac{1+\Phi_t}{1+\varphi}=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big\vert_{\mathcal F^S_t}\), where \(\varphi=\frac{p}{1-p}\) is the initial likelihood ratio. In the Wonham-filter case, the paper notes this identity fails, blocking the same reduction.

The core mathematical object of interest is the (dual) free boundary arising in the dual optimal stopping formulation of the investor’s mixed control/stopping problem under partial information; the paper’s integral-equation approach computes this boundary efficiently in the Bayesian-filter case by dimension reduction.
problem_definition_latex: Develop an analogue of the paper’s dimension-reduction technique (or an alternative tractable transformation) for the case where the unobservable drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter), so that the resulting free-boundary characterization does not require evaluating expectations under an unknown joint transition density and yields a numerically solvable integral equation (or comparably tractable characterization) for the free boundary.
latex:
Consider a continuous-time financial market on a filtered probability space supporting a risky asset price process \(S\) with volatility \(\sigma>0\), and an unobservable drift process \(\mu_t\) taking two values \(\mu_h>\mu_l\). The agent observes only the price filtration \(\mathcal F^S_t\) generated by \(S\), not \(\mu_t\) nor the driving Brownian motion.

In the paper’s baseline model, \(\mu\) is an \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable two-point random variable and the conditional probability \(P_t=\mathbb P(\mu=\mu_h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) satisfies a Bayesian-filter SDE of the form \(dP_t=\Theta P_t(1-P_t)d\widetilde B_t\), where \(\Theta=(\mu_h-\mu_l)/\sigma\) and \(\widetilde B\) is the innovation Brownian motion. The authors then introduce the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\) and a measure change \(\mathbb Q\) under which \(\Phi\) becomes a geometric Brownian motion, enabling a dimension reduction that converts an intractable free-boundary integral equation involving \(\mathbb E[\cdot]\) under the joint law of \((Y_t,P_t)\) into a solvable integral equation without unknown joint transition densities.

In the alternative setting highlighted in the conclusion, the unobservable drift is instead a hidden two-state continuous-time Markov chain \(\alpha_t\in\{h,l\}\) with generator
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
-\lambda_h & \lambda_h\\
\lambda_l & -\lambda_l
\end{pmatrix},\qquad \lambda_h,\lambda_l>0,
\]
and \(\mu_t=\mu(\alpha_t)\in\{\mu_h,\mu_l\}\). The conditional probability \(P_t:=\mathbb P(\alpha_t=h\mid\mathcal F^S_t)\) then follows the Wonham filter
\[
dP_t = (\lambda_l-(\lambda_h+\lambda_l)P_t)\,dt + \Theta P_t(1-P_t)\,d\widetilde B_t,
\]
with innovation process \(d\widetilde B_t = \sigma^{-1}dS_t/S_t - \mathbb E[\mu(\alpha_t)\mid\mathcal F^S_t]dt\).
Define again the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t:=\frac{P_t}{1-P_t}\). Under a measure \(\mathbb Q\) analogous to the one used for the Bayesian-filter case, \(\Phi\) satisfies an SDE of the form
\[
d\Phi_t = (\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)\,dt + \Theta\Phi_t\,dW_t,
\]
for a \(\mathbb Q\)-Brownian motion \(W\). In the Bayesian-filter case, a key identity enabling dimension reduction is \(F_t=\frac{1+\Phi_t}{1+\varphi}=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big\vert_{\mathcal F^S_t}\), where \(\varphi=\frac{p}{1-p}\) is the initial likelihood ratio. In the Wonham-filter case, the paper notes this identity fails, blocking the same reduction.

The core mathematical object of interest is the (dual) free boundary arising in the dual optimal stopping formulation of the investor’s mixed control/stopping problem under partial information; the paper’s integral-equation approach computes this boundary efficiently in the Bayesian-filter case by dimension reduction.

Develop an analogue of the paper’s dimension-reduction technique (or an alternative tractable transformation) for the case where the unobservable drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter), so that the resulting free-boundary characterization does not require evaluating expectations under an unknown joint transition density and yields a numerically solvable integral equation (or comparably tractable characterization) for the free boundary.
open_problem_quote: There remain some problems, for example, the current approach can not be extended if the drift coefficient is a hidden two-state Markov chain.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a methodological/generalization question rather than a falsifiable universal claim; there is no clear finite instance space where counterexamples would resolve the issue. One could test candidate reductions numerically, but failure on examples would not constitute a definitive counterexample to existence of some other approach.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically explore candidate measure changes, state augmentations, and transformations to simplify the Wonham-filter dynamics and then benchmark them by computing the resulting free boundary numerically on simulated paths. The fitness signal could be computational tractability/accuracy (e.g., runtime and agreement with Monte Carlo dynamic programming), but the search space is large and success depends on discovering the right analytic structure.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Xing–Ma–Zheng (2024), the key computational bottleneck is the Volterra free-boundary equation (their (3.9)) involving expectations under the joint law of the dual state \((Y_t,P_t)\). In the static two-point prior (Bayesian filter) case, they follow Klein’s likelihood-ratio idea: with \(\Phi_t=P_t/(1-P_t)\) and a Girsanov change of measure \(\mathbb Q\), \(\Phi\) becomes geometric Brownian motion and, crucially, \(F_t=(1+\Phi_t)/(1+\varphi)=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\vert_{\mathcal F_t^S}\). This identity allows them to rewrite the dual stopping problem so that one state variable becomes a parameter, leading to a numerically tractable one-dimensional integral equation (their (3.19)).

The only forward-citing work provided, “S-shaped Utility Maximization with VaR Constraint and Partial Information,” develops closely related integral representations under partial information but remains in the same Bayesian-filter (static drift) setting and again relies on the same martingale likelihood-ratio identity \(F_t=(1+\Phi_t)/(1+\phi)\). It therefore does not address the Wonham-filter case highlighted as open in Xing–Ma–Zheng’s conclusion, where \(P_t\) has mean-reverting drift and \(\Phi_t\) satisfies \(d\Phi_t=(\lambda_l-\lambda_h\Phi_t)(1+\Phi_t)dt+\Theta\Phi_t dW_t\) under \(\mathbb Q\), breaking the simple Radon–Nikodym relationship and blocking the dimension reduction.

Based on the supplied citation set, there is currently no paper that resolves the requested extension to hidden Markov-chain drift uncertainty. The problem remains open: one needs either a different measure-change/augmentation that restores a tractable density process, or an alternative characterization (e.g., coupled integral equations for regime-conditioned components, or a PDE/IE system exploiting the finite-state structure) that avoids unknown joint transition densities while retaining numerical efficiency comparable to the Bayesian-filter reduction.

sources.title: S-shaped Utility Maximization with VaR Constraint and Partial Information
sources.theorem_or_result: For a partial-information portfolio problem with unobservable drift \,\(\mu\in\{\mu_h,\mu_l\}\) (static two-point prior, Bayesian filter), the paper introduces a Girsanov change of measure \(\mathbb Q\) under which the likelihood ratio \(\Phi_t=(\hat\mu_t-\mu_l)/(\mu_h-\hat\mu_t)\) satisfies \(d\Phi_t=\Theta\Phi_t\,dW_t^{\mathbb Q}\) and \(F_t=(1+\Phi_t)/(1+\phi)=\frac{d\mathbb P}{d\mathbb Q}\big|_{\mathcal F_t^S}\). This yields a one-dimensional integral representation of the dual value function and constraint function (their (3.2)–(3.3)) and a constructive characterization of the optimal Lagrange multiplier/feasibility threshold (Theorem 3.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an analogue of the dimension-reduction/integral-equation method when the drift is a hidden two-state Markov chain (Wonham filter), where the key identity \(F_t=(1+\Phi_t)/(1+\phi)=d\mathbb P/d\mathbb Q\) fails. This citing paper does not treat the Wonham-filter case; it explicitly assumes \(\mu\) is \(\mathcal F_0\)-measurable with two-point prior and uses exactly the Bayesian-filter identity to reduce dimension and obtain integral representations. Thus it does not resolve the Markov-chain drift setting, but it provides a closely related measure-change template and highlights that the reduction hinges on the martingale likelihood-ratio structure absent in the Wonham case.
